item.title: A Continent as Stadium: What Makes the 2026 World Cup Unlike Any Before It
item.body:
# Soccer's Biggest Experiment

The FIFA World Cup has arrived in North America, and it is doing so at a scale the tournament has never attempted. For the first time, the event is co-hosted by three nations — the United States, Canada, and Mexico — and contested by an expanded field of 48 teams. The result is a competition stretched across a continent.

## Bigger in every dimension

The jump from 32 to 48 teams is the headline change. It means more matches, more host cities, and more nations getting their first taste of the sport's premier stage. For fans of countries that rarely qualified under the old format, this tournament is a genuine opening.

That expansion comes with logistical reality: teams and supporters travel enormous distances between venues spread across three countries and multiple time zones. The geography itself becomes part of the story.

## Three hosts, three flavors

What makes this edition distinctive is the contrast among its hosts:

- **The United States** brings massive stadiums and a sports-entertainment infrastructure built for events of this size.
- **Mexico** offers some of the most passionate, atmospheric soccer crowds on the planet, with a deep football culture and history of hosting.
- **Canada** steps onto the men's World Cup hosting stage in a way it never has before, putting the sport in front of new audiences.

Each host city stamps its own identity on the matches it holds, turning a single tournament into a tour of three distinct soccer cultures.

## Why it matters beyond the trophy

For North America, this is also a proving ground. A successful, well-organized tournament across three nations is the kind of showcase that can accelerate the sport's growth in markets where it has long competed for attention.

The expanded format has its skeptics — more teams can mean more lopsided early matches — but it also democratizes the event, rewarding regions that the old structure squeezed out.

## The takeaway

This World Cup is as much an experiment in scale and geography as it is a sporting contest. Whoever lifts the trophy, the lasting question is whether a tournament this large, spread this wide, can keep the intimacy and intensity that made the World Cup the world's biggest single-sport event in the first place. Early signs suggest the appetite is there.
